contents: Presentation Format Sample Exam Questions What You Take Away from This Course The Complete Pipeline Five Final Messages Grading Policy

items: git log --oneline — explain your development process
Show CI run (green checks on latest PR or main) — the automated proof
Show one test you wrote by hand — explain why it matters
Show one quality fix — explain the violation
Show one example where you corrected Claude Code’s output
Show your vibe coding whiteboard photo vs. constrained result — what changed?
Show CLAUDE.md and explain what rules you taught the AI
Show cognitive-core in action — a hook catching a violation, a skill you used, or your /fitness score

body.quote: The “corrected AI output” question is the most important. It proves you think critically.

items: Weight breakdown: see Grading Policy
Explain the difference between git add and git commit. Why does the staging area exist?
What does Maven do that javac alone cannot? Name 3 capabilities.
Why would PMD flag an empty catch block? What’s the real-world risk?
Explain the AAA pattern. Why should every test follow it?
What is a trust boundary in AI-assisted development? Give one example from your project.
Given a broken pom.xml, identify and fix the errors
Write a JUnit test for a given method, covering at least one edge case
Read a Jenkinsfile and explain what each stage does
Explain the difference between probabilistic rules (CLAUDE.md) and deterministic enforcement (hooks). Give one example of each.
What is MCP? Why should an agent have at most 4–5 tools? What happens with more?
Describe the agentic loop. What are the two possible stop_reason values and what does each mean?
Explain how your project’s pipeline maps to the production agent architecture. Name at least 3 architecture patterns you used and where.
NEW: Draw the agentic loop and explain how Claude Code uses it when you ask it to fix a bug.
NEW: Explain the three layers of cognitive-core (hooks, skills, agents). Which layer is deterministic and which is probabilistic?

title: What You Take Away from This Course

body.code: Before this course: "I learned some Java" After this course: "I can build, test, review, and deliver Java projects professionally, with AI assistance where it helps, human judgment where it matters, and architectural vocabulary that connects to production systems."

You + Claude Code + cognitive-core | Git ........................... audit trail + provenance | + validate-bash hook (branch guard) Maven ......................... capability contracts | + tool risk classification PMD/SpotBugs/CheckStyle ....... regression gates | + post-edit-lint hook (automatic) JUnit/Mockito ................. eval datasets | + /test-scaffold + /fitness score Jenkins/Nexus ................. traces + SLOs | + graduated fitness gates (60→95%) Security awareness ............ trust boundaries + perimeter | + validate-write + Source Authority T1-T5 Architecture .................. control plane + tool gateway + memory | + @code-standards-reviewer + parsimony PROFESSIONAL DEVELOPER WITH ARCHITECTURAL THINKING
body.note: Prompt Engineering (Module 7) shapes every stage — it is the how, not a where. You prompt to generate, refactor, review, and debug at every row above.

rows: AI writes the diff; you sign the commit. Git records who decided — not who typed.
pom.xml is a contract, not config. Classify every Maven command by blast radius.
CLAUDE.md is a suggestion AI may ignore. CheckStyle / PMD / SpotBugs are gates it cannot.
Tests are your AI eval dataset. One edge case beats a hundred trivial assertions.
Stop the line when the build is red. A pipeline with weak gates is just theater.
You draw the trust boundary; AI won’t. No secrets in, no irreversible actions out unsupervised.
Show, don’t tell: examples beat polite instructions. Structured output is the bridge from chat to CI.
Production AI is familiar at every layer: tests become evals, linters become guardrails, CI becomes orchestration.
Deterministic hooks beat probabilistic prompts. Install cognitive-core in the capstone — don’t hand-roll guardrails.

title: Grading Policy
desc: Base grade 1.0. Violations subtract; bonuses add.

body.card: Penalties — each violation subtracts from base

rows: Not formatted per Google Java Style (eclipse-java-google-style.xml)
mvn clean install fails or reports errors
Contains generated files, IDE config, or log files
Missing assertions, empty lifecycle methods, or no JUnit plugin in pom.xml
No Mockito examples in the test suite
Missing or empty — no project-specific rules for AI
Cannot explain why the code exists or how it works — shipped AI output you don’t understand
All 9 patterns from Module 10 evidenced (git log, ADRs, PlantUML, etc.)
You built a reusable slash command or skill
table.note: Max bonus: +1.1. Bonuses cannot compensate for missing fundamentals.
